# ADR-001 — Default Tech Stack (Next.js + Supabase + Vercel)

**Status:** Adopted  
**Date:** 2026-05-26  
**Deciders:** Solution Architect (solution-architect), CEO Command Squad  
**Supersedes:** —  
**Superseded by:** —

---

## Decision

The devosnt App Factory adopts **Next.js + Supabase + Vercel** as its default production stack for all new business applications. This stack is used unless an app's requirements explicitly demand otherwise (see Reversal Conditions below). Every new app begins with this baseline; deviations require a written ADR from the Solution Architect approved by the CEO Command Squad before any build work starts.

---

## Layer-by-Layer Rationale

### Frontend — Next.js (App Router)
- Full-stack React framework with SSR, SSG, and API routes in one deployment unit.
- App Router enables per-route caching, streaming, and React Server Components — critical for dashboard-heavy business apps.
- Built-in image optimisation, i18n routing, and middleware — reduces need for bespoke infra.
- RTL/Arabic support is CSS-level; no framework constraint.
- Large ecosystem: Shadcn/ui, Radix, Tailwind — ships design systems fast.

### Backend / API — Next.js API Routes + Supabase Edge Functions
- API routes colocated with frontend eliminate a separate backend service for CRUD-heavy apps.
- Supabase Edge Functions (Deno) handle webhooks, background triggers, and heavy compute without a separate server.
- TypeScript end-to-end: shared types between frontend and backend; fewer runtime surprises.

### Database — Supabase (PostgreSQL)
- Postgres: the most mature open-source RDBMS; full ACID, JSON support, RLS, triggers, foreign keys.
- Row-Level Security (RLS) enforced at the DB layer — auth cannot be accidentally bypassed by a bad API route.
- Supabase wraps Postgres with an auto-generated REST + GraphQL API, reducing boilerplate for simple reads.
- Realtime subscriptions via Postgres logical replication — live dashboards and KDS screens without a separate message broker.
- Migrations via `supabase db diff` — version-controlled schema, reproducible across environments.
- Free tier for prototypes; predictable pricing for production.

### Auth — Supabase Auth (GoTrue)
- Supabase Auth is tightly coupled to the DB: `auth.uid()` is available inside RLS policies, making row-ownership trivial.
- Supports email/password, magic link, OAuth (Google, Apple, GitHub), and phone OTP out of the box.
- JWT access tokens with configurable expiry; refresh tokens stored server-side — no custom token infra needed.
- Custom claims via a `users` or `profiles` table + JWT hook for role-based access control (RBAC).

### Deploy — Vercel
- Git-push deploys: every PR gets a preview URL, main gets a production URL — zero DevOps overhead for most apps.
- Edge Network: assets and API routes served from PoPs closest to users; critical for Kuwait-based users hitting GCC-region Supabase.
- Environment variable management per environment (preview, staging, production) — no secrets in code.
- Serverless functions auto-scale; no capacity planning for spiky traffic.
- Integrates natively with Next.js: automatic ISR, image CDN, middleware edge runtime.

### Mobile — React Native + Expo (when required)
- Default is web-first (Next.js). When a native mobile app is required, React Native + Expo is the preferred choice.
- Shared business logic and API client code reused from the web project.
- Expo EAS Build handles iOS/Android CI without a Mac/Windows build machine.
- Not installed by default — a mobile sub-issue is created only when the PRD explicitly requires native mobile.

### Payments — MyFatoorah (Kuwait) / Stripe (International)
- MyFatoorah for KNet and local payment methods (GCC-regulated apps). Integrated via server-side API route.
- Stripe for international payments where KNet is not required; Stripe's SDK is best-in-class.
- Payment processing never in browser JS; always a Next.js API route or Supabase Edge Function.
- PCI scope minimised: payment form hosted by gateway (hosted fields / checkout); no card data touches our servers.

### Messaging / Notifications — Resend (email) + Twilio (SMS) + Supabase Realtime (in-app)
- Resend: transactional email with React Email templates; reliable deliverability, simple API.
- Twilio: SMS and WhatsApp for OTPs, alerts, and order notifications (Kuwait numbers supported).
- Supabase Realtime: in-app push via Postgres changes — no additional broker for live notification feeds.
- Push notifications (mobile): Expo Notifications + FCM/APNs when a native mobile layer exists.

### Analytics — PostHog (product analytics) + Supabase Views (operational reports)
- PostHog: open-source product analytics; self-hostable; captures events, funnels, session recordings, feature flags.
- Supabase Views + Materialized Views: operational KPIs (daily orders, revenue, inventory) queried directly from the DB — no ETL for simple reports.
- For heavy BI: Metabase connected to Supabase read replica (see data-warehouse-analytics skill for CDC pipeline).

---

## Alternatives Considered

| Layer | Alternative | Reason Not Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| Frontend | Remix | Smaller ecosystem; less tooling for our skill set; Vercel optimises Next.js preferentially |
| Frontend | Vue / Nuxt | Team and skills library are React-first; lower component ecosystem |
| Frontend | SvelteKit | Compelling but too niche for rapid onboarding of new agents/devs |
| Backend | Express.js / Fastify (standalone) | Adds a separate service to deploy and monitor; Next.js API routes cover most CRUD needs |
| Backend | NestJS | Overengineered for most apps; adds boilerplate without benefit at early scale |
| Database | Firebase Firestore | NoSQL means no RLS on relational data; schema drift is harder to audit; cost scales badly |
| Database | PlanetScale (MySQL) | No RLS; branching workflow adds complexity; Postgres ecosystem is richer |
| Database | MongoDB Atlas | Document model doesn't suit financial / relational business data |
| Auth | Auth0 | $$$; external JWT means no native `auth.uid()` in Supabase RLS; extra hop |
| Auth | Clerk | Good DX but same RLS coupling problem as Auth0; cost scales with MAU |
| Auth | Custom JWT | High maintenance; security risk; not justified for most apps |
| Deploy | AWS Amplify | More complex; less Next.js-native; slower preview deployments |
| Deploy | Railway / Render | Good for Node APIs but not optimised for Next.js App Router ISR/Edge |
| Deploy | Self-hosted k8s | Operational overhead far exceeds benefit at current scale |
| Payments | Tap Payments | Less documentation and SDK maturity vs MyFatoorah for KNet |
| Analytics | Mixpanel | Proprietary; cost at scale; PostHog is open-source with equivalent feature set |
| Analytics | Amplitude | Same tradeoff as Mixpanel |

---

## Reversal Conditions

This stack should be reconsidered (trigger a new ADR) when ANY of the following are true:

1. **Frontend:** An app requires a native desktop experience (Electron/Tauri), a game-like canvas-based UI, or a heavily SEO-independent SPA where bundle size is the dominant concern.
2. **Backend/API:** The app has stateful long-running processes (WebSockets for real-time gaming, complex job orchestration) that don't fit the serverless execution model.
3. **Database:** Throughput exceeds ~10 k writes/sec on a single Supabase project (time-series data, IoT streams), making a dedicated TSDB (TimescaleDB, ClickHouse) more appropriate.
4. **Auth:** A regulated industry requirement (banking, insurance) mandates on-premise identity or a specific certified IdP that Supabase Auth cannot integrate with.
5. **Deploy:** Monthly Vercel bill exceeds $2,000 for a single project (traffic or seat ceiling hit); evaluate self-hosted Next.js on Railway or containerised deploy.
6. **Mobile:** The app is primarily mobile (>80% usage) with heavy device API usage (BLE, NFC, offline-first) where Expo's managed workflow is insufficient and bare React Native or Flutter is required.
7. **Payments:** A geography outside GCC/International (e.g., India-first app) mandates a local gateway not supported by MyFatoorah or Stripe.
8. **Stack-wide:** A successor framework reaches maturity with measurably better DX + deployment economics and the team has evaluated it in a real build (not just a benchmark).

---

## Current Status

**Adopted** — in use for all devosnt App Factory builds as of 2026-05-26. This ADR is the baseline for monthly architecture reviews. Any proposed deviation must reference this document and state which reversal condition applies.

---

## Review Schedule

This ADR is reviewed quarterly (next: 2026-09-01) or when a reversal condition is triggered. The Solution Architect owns updates.

---

## Cross-References

- ADR-002 (DEV-37): App Hub Model — issue lifecycle and artifact organisation
- CLAUDE.md: `solution-architect` prompt, line: *"Default stack is Next.js + Supabase + Vercel unless the app demands otherwise"*
- Monthly Architecture Review: this ADR is the canonical stack baseline for all drift assessments
issues.title: ADR-001 — Default Tech Stack (Next.js + Supabase + Vercel)

## Skill Registration

**Multica Skill ID:** `035f76ed-3f96-42bd-91ef-152d11d359b2`
**Name:** `tdd-mastery`
**Source:** rohitg00/awesome-claude-code-toolkit → `skills/tdd-mastery/SKILL.md` (Apache 2.0)
**Imported by:** DEV-105

## Purpose
Red-Green-Refactor TDD cycle enforcement across all devosnt code tasks. Enforces 80% line / 75% branch coverage thresholds in CI. Pairs with `qa-test-plan-generator` at the QA gate.

## Stack Coverage
- **Primary:** TypeScript / Next.js (Vitest preferred, Jest fallback)
- **Secondary:** Python (pytest + coverage.py)
- **Tertiary:** Go (table-driven tests, go tool cover)

## Coverage Thresholds (wired into QA acceptance criteria)
- Line coverage: ≥ 80%
- Branch coverage: ≥ 75%
- Excludes: `*.generated.ts`, `*.d.ts`, `next.config.*`, `tailwind.config.*`, migration files

## Integration
- **qa-test-plan-generator**: Every checklist item from qa-test-plan-generator must map to a passing automated test or be explicitly noted as manual-only.
- **Build Squad**: Assumed active on any issue producing business logic, API routes, or data-mutation code.
- **QA Gate**: Coverage below threshold blocks `in_review → done`; triggers a `TEST:` sub-issue.

## Attribution
Apache 2.0 — original by rohitg00 (https://github.com/rohitg00/awesome-claude-code-toolkit)

## Improvement Rule
Review after 5 uses: count production bugs TDD would have caught, update language patterns, retire irrelevant anti-patterns.
issues.title: SKILL: tdd-mastery — Red-Green-Refactor TDD with coverage enforcement

## SECURITY REVIEW: sandbox-runtime import

**Dispatch sub-issue:** [DEV-117](mention://issue/9bdb8397-efee-4d72-ab46-cacde1b81ba8)
**Source URL:** https://github.com/anthropic-experimental/sandbox-runtime
**License:** Apache 2.0

### What to review
Anthropic's OS-level agent sandbox tool (`srt`). Restricts filesystem and network access on arbitrary processes using native OS primitives.

**Install footprint:**
- `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime`
- TypeScript/Node.js; no persistent daemon
- Invoked per-command: `srt "<command>"`

**Platform specifics (our environment):**
- **Linux (workspace):** Uses `bubblewrap` — requires `bwrap` binary and `user_namespaces` kernel feature
- **macOS:** Uses `sandbox-exec` (not relevant for workspace)
- **Network:** Proxy-based network filtering with configurable allowlist

**Required scopes / permissions:**
- Process execution with restricted namespaces (Linux user namespaces)
- No persistent disk writes beyond the sandboxed process
- No external API calls; purely local

**Specific areas to audit:**
1. Does bubblewrap require SUID or privileged capabilities in our Docker-based workspace?
2. Are the default allowlist defaults restrictive enough (which paths/hosts are allowed by default)?
3. Can the allowlist configuration be bypassed or injected by a malicious skill?
4. Supply chain audit: npm package `@anthropic-ai/sandbox-runtime` on npmjs.com — verify package provenance.
5. Are there known CVEs or privilege escalation paths in bubblewrap for our kernel version (6.8)?

**When done:**
- Pin `decision=green` or `decision=red` on this issue
- If green: run `multica issue status 9bdb8397-efee-4d72-ab46-cacde1b81ba8 todo` to promote the dispatch sub-issue
- If red: pin `blocked_reason=security_review_failed` on DEV-117 and leave it backlog
issues.title: SECURITY REVIEW: sandbox-runtime import

## SECURITY REVIEW: mcp-memory-service import

**Dispatch sub-issue:** [DEV-116](mention://issue/deb94651-8a6d-4630-bf8a-d833cca213ac)
**Source URL:** https://github.com/doobidoo/mcp-memory-service
**License:** Apache 2.0

### What to review
Python MCP server providing persistent agent memory via sqlite-vec + REST API.

**Install footprint:**
- `pip install mcp-memory-service`
- Runs as local daemon; optional Docker image
- Exposes HTTP REST API (configurable port) + MCP stdio transport

**Required scopes / permissions:**
- **Filesystem:** Reads/writes sqlite-vec database to local disk (configurable path)
- **Network:** Listens on a local HTTP port; no external outbound calls in core path
- **Background tasks:** Autonomy consolidation scheduler runs in background thread

**Specific areas to audit:**
1. Can the consolidation scheduler be triggered to write arbitrary data to disk?
2. Does the HTTP REST API expose authentication controls, or is it open by default?
3. Are there any supply chain risks in the PyPI package (`mcp-memory-service`)?
4. Does the knowledge graph traversal have injection risks?
5. Any OWASP-relevant patterns in the API surface (memory injection, data exfil via graph)?

**When done:**
- Pin `decision=green` or `decision=red` on this issue
- If green: run `multica issue status deb94651-8a6d-4630-bf8a-d833cca213ac todo` to promote the dispatch sub-issue
- If red: pin `blocked_reason=security_review_failed` on DEV-116 and leave it backlog

# SKILL: multi-agent-coordinator

**Name:** multi-agent-coordinator  
**Model:** opus  
**Source:** [VoltAgent/awesome-claude-code-subagents — 09-meta-orchestration/multi-agent-coordinator.md](https://github.com/VoltAgent/awesome-claude-code-subagents) (MIT, security-cleared via DEV-63)  
**Version:** 1.0.0 — 2026-05-26

---

## Purpose

Coordinate concurrent agents working across multiple Multica issues — map dependency graphs onto parent/child issue hierarchies, prevent deadlock via the `blocked`-status workflow, route work through issue-based handoff, and apply fault tolerance aligned with the `agent-self-healing-policy` skill.

Use when a parent issue spawns three or more parallel sub-issues that have shared dependencies, ordering constraints, or synchronization requirements that cannot be resolved by a single assignee reading the issue description alone.

---

## Trigger

Invoke this skill when **any two** of the following are true for a work package:

1. Three or more parallel sub-issues exist under the same parent and at least one must wait for another to reach `in_review` or `done` before it can start.
2. Two or more squads own different pieces of the same feature and must exchange an artifact (schema, API contract, component) before one can unblock the other.
3. A previous parallel run caused a merge conflict, duplicate work, or a race condition on a shared resource (DB schema, env-var file, shared config).
4. The orchestrating agent (Sprint Commander or CEO Command Squad) explicitly delegates coordination to this skill.

---

## Required Input

| Field | Source | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Parent issue ID | Provided by caller | `MUL-4333e896` |
| List of sub-issue IDs and their titles | `multica issue list --parent <parent-id>` | `DEV-100`, `DEV-101`, `DEV-102` |
| Dependency declaration | Issue descriptions or explicit caller note | "DEV-101 requires DEV-100's schema to be in `in_review` first" |
| Squad/agent assignments per sub-issue | Issue metadata or assignee fields | `build-agent` on DEV-100, `db-agent` on DEV-101 |
| Shared resources (if any) | Caller or architecture docs | Supabase schema file, `.env.example`, shared API contract |

---

## Steps

### Phase 1 — Dependency Graph Construction

1. Read all sub-issues under the parent (`multica issue list --parent <parent-id> --output json`).
2. Parse each sub-issue's **Dependencies** field and comments for inter-issue blocking claims.
3. Build a directed acyclic graph (DAG) where each node is a sub-issue and each directed edge means "source must reach `in_review` or `done` before target can move to `todo`".
4. Run topological sort. If a cycle is detected, it is a **deadlock** — go to Phase 2 immediately.
5. Emit a plain-text dependency table in your first comment on the parent issue:

```
Dependency order (topological):
  [start] DEV-100 (no deps)
  [start] DEV-103 (no deps)
  [wait]  DEV-101 → unblocks when DEV-100 reaches in_review
  [wait]  DEV-102 → unblocks when DEV-101 reaches done
```

6. Set `--status todo` only for issues with no incoming edges. Set `--status backlog` for all others.

### Phase 2 — Deadlock Detection and Prevention

A **deadlock** in Multica terms is any cycle in the dependency graph (A waits for B, B waits for A), or a situation where two agents each hold a shared resource lock and both are waiting for the other's resource.

Detection:
- Topological sort failure → structural deadlock.
- Two issues both in `blocked` status citing each other as the blocker → runtime deadlock.

Resolution:
1. Identify the minimum edge to cut from the cycle (prefer cutting the dependency with the least work remaining).
2. Create a new sub-issue that produces the shared artifact independently (no other dependencies).
3. Re-route both blocked issues to depend on this new sub-issue instead of each other.
4. Set both originally-blocked issues to `backlog`; set the new resolver issue to `todo`.
5. Post a comment on the parent explaining the restructuring.

Prevention rules (enforce these when creating sub-issues):
- Never create two issues that each list the other in their **Dependencies** field.
- Shared resources (schema files, env files, API contracts) must be owned by exactly one issue at a time; all consumers list that issue as a dependency, not each other.
- If the dependency chain is longer than 4 hops, escalate to the CEO Command Squad before proceeding.

### Phase 3 — Message Routing (Issue-Based Handoff)

Multica's handoff primitive is a sub-issue + assignee mention, not a direct message. Route work as follows:

| Scenario | Action |
|---|---|
| Agent A's output is needed by Agent B | When A reaches `in_review`, add a comment on B's issue: "DEV-100 is now `in_review`. Artifact: `<url or description>`. You may proceed." Then promote B to `todo`. |
| Agent A produces an artifact consumed by multiple agents | Post the artifact link on the parent issue (not each child) so all consumers see it in one canonical place. |
| Broadcast (all agents need to know something) | Comment on the parent issue; do not mention every agent individually — they will re-read on their next trigger. |
| Escalation to a human | Use `[@Name](mention://member/<user-id>)` on the relevant issue. One mention per escalation; no follow-up mention unless the human has not responded after the stated SLA. |
| First-touch delegation to an agent | Use `[@AgentName](mention://agent/<agent-id>)` in the first delegation comment only. Never re-mention the same agent to acknowledge or thank — that triggers a loop. |

### Phase 4 — Fault Tolerance and Recovery

Aligned with the `agent-self-healing-policy` skill:

| Failure Mode | Detection | Recovery |
|---|---|---|
| Agent run fails (non-zero exit, timeout) | `agent-self-healing-policy` triggers auto-retry on a different model | Coordinator re-reads the issue after 10 minutes; if still `in_progress` with no new comment, escalate to CEO Command Squad |
| Agent posts `blocked` status | Issue status = `blocked`, comment present | Read the `blocked_reason` metadata or comment; resolve the blocker or create a resolver sub-issue (Phase 2); re-promote to `todo` |
| Agent produces wrong artifact (QA rejects) | Issue moved back from `in_review` to `in_progress` | Re-read the QA rejection comment; create a correction sub-issue if the scope is more than a 1-hour fix; otherwise reassign in-place |
| Shared resource race (two agents edit same file) | Merge conflict in PR | Whichever PR was merged second owns resolution; create a cleanup sub-issue assigned to the same agent |
| Coordinator self-failure (this skill crashes) | Parent issue stuck in `in_progress` with no comment update for > 2 h | CEO Command Squad re-triggers this skill on the parent issue from scratch; all sub-issue states are re-read as ground truth |

Circuit-breaker rule: If three consecutive recovery attempts on the same sub-issue fail, set that issue to `blocked`, pin `blocked_reason = "3 consecutive recovery failures — human review required"` in metadata, and escalate to the workspace owner.

### Phase 5 — Parallel Execution Governance

Enforce these invariants throughout the coordination lifecycle:

1. **One assignee per issue.** Never assign two agents to the same issue simultaneously.
2. **Shared resources serialized.** Issues that write to the same file or DB table must be in a serial dependency chain, not parallel.
3. **Status discipline.** An issue moves `todo → in_progress → in_review → done`. Backwards moves (`in_review → in_progress`) are valid (QA rejection); skipping steps is not.
4. **Parent stays open.** The parent issue does not move to `done` until every child is `done` or `cancelled` with a documented reason.
5. **No ghost work.** If a sub-issue becomes unnecessary after another issue completes (e.g., a dependency produced a superset), cancel it with `multica issue status <id> cancelled` and post a comment explaining why.

### Phase 6 — Monitoring and Completion

Poll the parent issue's children on each re-trigger:

```
For each child issue:
  if status == done or cancelled: mark as resolved
  if status == blocked: apply Phase 4 recovery
  if status == in_review and dependents exist: promote dependents to todo (Phase 3)
  if status == in_progress and last_comment > 2h ago: flag for health check

If all children are done or cancelled:
  post final summary comment on parent (see Output section)
  move parent to in_review
```

---

## Output

### Per-run comment on the parent issue

```
**Coordination status — <timestamp>**

Resolved: DEV-100 (done), DEV-103 (done)
In progress: DEV-101 (in_progress, build-agent, no blockers)
Waiting: DEV-102 (backlog, unblocks when DEV-101 reaches in_review)
Blocked: none

Next actions:
- DEV-101 expected in_review by EOD; DEV-102 will be promoted then.
- No deadlocks detected.
```

### Final summary comment when all children complete

```
**Multi-agent coordination complete — <parent-issue-id>**

Total sub-issues: N
  Completed: X
  Cancelled: Y (reasons documented on each issue)
  Escalations: Z (list)

Shared resources: <list of files/tables touched and who owns the final state>
Duration: <start → end>
Lessons / patterns to extract: <any reusable coordination pattern observed>
```

---

## Quality Checklist

Before closing out any coordination run, verify:

- [ ] Every sub-issue is in `done`, `cancelled` (with reason), or actively progressing with no stale blockers.
- [ ] No two issues are simultaneously in `in_progress` writing to the same shared resource.
- [ ] No cycle exists in the current dependency graph.
- [ ] Every `blocked` issue has a `blocked_reason` comment and a recovery action assigned.
- [ ] The parent issue has an up-to-date coordination status comment.
- [ ] No agent has been mentioned more than once for the same delegation (loop-prevention).
- [ ] All cancelled issues have a documented reason in the issue comments.
- [ ] Escalations to CEO Command Squad or workspace owner are documented on the parent.

---

## Failure Handling

| Failure | Immediate action |
|---|---|
| Cannot list sub-issues (CLI error) | Retry once; if fails again, post `blocked` on parent with `blocked_reason = "multica CLI unavailable"` and page workspace owner |
| Cycle detected in dependency graph | Apply Phase 2 restructuring; if cycle persists after restructuring, escalate to CEO Command Squad |
| Three recovery failures on one issue | Set `blocked`, pin metadata, escalate to workspace owner |
| Coordination comment fails to post | Retry with `--content-file` flag using a temp file path |
| Dependency chain > 4 hops | Escalate to CEO Command Squad before proceeding; do not auto-restructure deep chains |

---

## Improvement Rule

After every 5 uses of this skill, the Skill Builder agent reviews:

1. Were any dependency cycles detected? What structural pattern caused them?
2. Were the topological-sort phase outputs accurate enough for agents to act without re-reading this skill?
3. Did the fault-tolerance phase catch all failures within the 2-hour window?
4. Did any mention cause an unintended agent loop? Tighten the routing rules if so.
5. Update this skill file with refined rules; bump the version field; post the revision notes on the skill's library issue.

Retire this skill if Multica adds native dependency tracking between issues — at that point the DAG construction phase becomes redundant.

---

## Coordination Patterns Reference (Adapted to Multica)

| Pattern | Multica implementation |
|---|---|
| Master-worker | One parent issue (master) with N child issues (workers); coordinator promotes children sequentially or in parallel per DAG |
| Pipeline | Serial chain: each child's `in_review` status triggers the next child's `todo` promotion |
| Scatter-gather | All leaves set to `todo` simultaneously; coordinator waits for all to reach `done`; posts aggregated result on parent |
| Publish-subscribe | Shared artifact posted as comment on parent; each consuming agent reads from parent comment, not from the producer's issue |
| Circuit breaker | Three failures → `blocked` + escalate; no further retries until human clears the block |
| Fork-join | Parent forks at issue creation (all children `todo`); joins when all children reach `done` and coordinator posts final summary |

# COMPETITIVE BRIEF: LOVABLE
**Run date:** 2026-05-26

## What They Do
Lovable (lovable.dev) is an AI-powered full-stack app builder that generates production-ready React/TypeScript/Supabase applications from plain English prompts, eliminating the need to write code or configure visual builders. The platform targets non-technical founders, startup teams, and product managers who need a working web app for validation or MVP launch quickly. Its core value proposition is delivering a complete, deployable system — frontend UI, database schema, authentication, and payment integrations — in hours rather than weeks, with full GitHub code export so the user owns the output.

## Onboarding Flow
1. **Landing page chatbot** — Visitors describe an app idea and see live AI output *before* creating an account, proving capability with zero commitment.
2. **Email-only signup** — Five-second registration; no password required, no verification delay.
3. **Personalization survey** — Minimal discovery questions to tailor output; no template picker.
4. **Guided integration prompts** — Supabase (DB), Stripe (payments), and OAuth providers are introduced in context as each becomes relevant, not all at once.
5. **Real-time task narration** — The builder narrates AI subtasks ("Create schema → Compose UI → Spin preview") with an inline credit counter visible throughout.

Notable UX choices: share buttons are public-by-default to encourage viral loops; credit remaining is surfaced inline to avoid surprise bills.

## Pricing (as of 2026-05-26)
| Tier | Price | Credits/month | Notable limits |
|---|---|---|---|
| Free | $0 | 30 (5/day cap) | 5 lovable.app subdomains, private projects |
| Pro | $25/mo | 100 | Custom domains, unlimited collaborators |
| Business | $50/mo | 100 | SSO, team workspace, design templates, opt-out of data training |
| Enterprise | Custom | Custom | Volume pricing, SCIM, dedicated support |

Credit consumption varies by complexity: simple styling ~0.5 credits; a full landing page 2+ credits. Every prompt (including AI-introduced bug fixes) consumes credits. Student discount: up to 50% off Pro.

## Strengths
1. **Full-stack generation with code ownership** — GitHub two-way sync exports standard React/TypeScript/Tailwind code; users can deploy anywhere and fork freely after export. ([Lovable FAQ](https://lovable.dev/faq/code-export), [Export Guide](https://www.alanbagi.com/blog/can-i-export-an-lovable-project-guide))
2. **Auto-generated Supabase schema + RLS** — Plain-English prompts produce SQL schemas, table relationships, and Row-Level Security policies simultaneously with UI bindings and real-time subscriptions, eliminating manual backend setup. ([Lovable Supabase Integration](https://uibakery.io/blog/lovable-supabase-integration), [Docs](https://docs.lovable.dev/integrations/supabase))
3. **Agentic mode with 91% fewer errors** — Agent Mode makes multi-file changes and validates them with built-in browser testing and edge function validation before delivery. ([Lovable Blog](https://lovable.dev/blog/setting-the-standard-for-agentic-development), [Docs](https://docs.lovable.dev/features/agent-mode))

## Weaknesses
1. **Credit trap on AI-introduced bugs** — Every prompt costs credits, including re-fixes of bugs the AI introduced. Users report 70% of spend goes to fixing AI mistakes; costs of $200–$3,000+ for moderately complex apps are documented. The AI lacks persistent cross-session context, causing fixed bugs to resurface.
2. **Security vulnerabilities in generated code** — February 2026: 16 vulnerabilities (6 critical) found in a single Lovable-hosted app exposing 18,000 users' data. A 2026 audit found 10%+ of sampled production apps with live vulnerabilities. Generated code lacks rate limiting, CSRF tokens, and proper secrets management. ([The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/02/27/lovable_app_vulnerabilities/))
3. **Scaling failures past MVP** — Multi-step workflows and complex business logic confuse the AI. Data structures are tightly coupled and break when requirements change. Code that works in Lovable fails locally due to sync/build issues. Not suitable for enterprise or long-term applications.

## Patterns to Copy
1. **Value-before-signup chatbot** — Visitors see working AI output on the landing page before creating an account, proving capability and pre-qualifying intent. | Implementation note: build a public "describe your app" demo that generates a mockup or screen list without requiring login; capture emails at the output step.
2. **Transparent inline cost display** — Credits remaining are visible contextually during every action, not buried in a settings page. | Implementation note: surface usage/quota inline wherever a user takes a resource-consuming action; frame it as "X credits remaining" not as a warning.
3. **Agentic task narration UI** — Real-time narration of AI subtasks ("Designing schema → Wiring UI → Running tests") maintains psychological momentum on slow operations. | Implementation note: stream agent step labels into the UI for any long-running build or generation task; a spinner alone is insufficient.

## Patterns to Beat
1. **Per-message credit burn with no refund on AI failures** — Lovable charges credits for every prompt regardless of outcome, trapping users in expensive debug loops. | Our differentiation: charge credits only at verifiable checkpoints (compilation, test pass, deploy-ready). Auto-refund or credit-reverse when the AI re-introduces a previously-fixed bug (detected via diff comparison).
2. **Stateless agent with no cross-project memory** — Lovable's Agent Mode processes each session in isolation; design systems and naming conventions must be re-explained for every new project. | Our differentiation: maintain a persistent workspace memory layer that learns project conventions, component libraries, and schema patterns across all user projects and applies them proactively to new builds.
3. **No security hardening by default** — Generated apps ship without rate limiting, input validation, audit logging, or proper secrets management. | Our differentiation: auto-inject a security scaffold (helmet.js / equivalent, input sanitisation, secrets via env var, audit log stub) on every generated backend; run an automated pre-deploy security report with a one-click "fix all" option.

## Decision
**Study only** — Lovable has strong UX patterns worth adopting (value-before-signup, transparent costs, agentic narration) but its credit-trap pricing, documented security failures, and scaling ceiling make it a cautionary example rather than a source to import or adapt directly; our differentiation should explicitly solve the problems that cause Lovable users to outgrow the platform.

## Sources
- [Lovable FAQ — Code Export](https://lovable.dev/faq/code-export)
- [Lovable Agent Mode Docs](https://docs.lovable.dev/features/agent-mode)
- [Lovable Agent Blog](https://lovable.dev/blog/setting-the-standard-for-agentic-development)
- [Lovable Supabase Integration](https://uibakery.io/blog/lovable-supabase-integration)
- [Lovable Pricing 2026](https://flowith.io/blog/lovable-pricing-2026-free-vs-starter-vs-pro/)
- [Lovable Pricing & Credits](https://www.banani.co/blog/lovable-pricing-and-credits)
- [Lovable Onboarding Teardown](https://www.candu.ai/blog/lovables-agentic-onboarding)
- [Lovable App Exposed 18K Users — The Register](https://www.theregister.com/2026/02/27/lovable_app_vulnerabilities/)
- [Lovable Bugs & Realistic Costs 2026](https://forasoft.medium.com/lovable-app-bugs-how-to-fix-them-when-to-hire-developers-realistic-costs-2026-946199c6496d)
- [Lovable Limitations Guide](https://p0stman.com/guides/lovable-limitations)
- [Why Startups Outgrow Lovable](https://www.fastdev.com/blog/blog/startups-scaleups-lovable-limitations/)
- [Lovable Review 2026](https://weavai.app/blog/en/2026/04/26/2026-lovable-review-is-this-ai-app-builder-worth-it/)

## Purpose
OWASP-aware code review covering logic errors, vulnerability patterns, complexity analysis, and best practices. Acts as the first gate before `security-review-checklist` on the `in_review → done` transition in the App Factory workflow. Run on Opus.

## Input
Workspace Upgrade Report — 2026-05-26, action #1. The orphan skill `self-hosted-supabase-tunnel` documents a free demo-backend path "when production Supabase Cloud credentials are not yet provisioned," but no agent owns it and `one-prompt-to-app` never reaches for it. As a result, DEV-48 (`landing-page-builder` reference demo) sits blocked on `waiting_on=admin_credentials_and_hosting`.

## Expected Output
A memory/default-policy update so that future CEO + `one-prompt-to-app` runs auto-pick the self-hosted-supabase-tunnel demo backend when production Supabase credentials are absent. Concretely:

- One **default-decision memory entry** captured in CEO's persistent memory (or the equivalent workspace memory layer you maintain) stating: *"If a build's parent issue lacks Supabase Cloud credentials AND has no `waiting_on=admin_credentials_*` set, default to `self-hosted-supabase-tunnel` instead of escalating. Owner: CEO."*
- A short note attached to the `one-prompt-to-app` skill (or queued for skill-builder to merge under action #2) so the orchestrator references the tunnel as the demo-backend default.
- Stale-fact audit: scan `memory list`/equivalent for any pinned fact that says "blocked on Supabase credentials = wait" and replace with the new default.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] New memory entry exists and is retrievable by `ceo` on the next run.
- [ ] At least one obsolete "waiting on Supabase credentials" memory entry is either reconfirmed or removed.
- [ ] Memory entry cites this issue and DEV-48 as evidence.

## Dependencies
- Sibling action #2 (skill-builder) attaches `self-hosted-supabase-tunnel` to its owner agent; this memory entry references that skill by name so the link survives even before the attachment lands.

## Risk
Low. Worst case: CEO defaults to the tunnel for an app that already had Supabase Cloud provisioned. Mitigation: add the conditional ("AND has no Supabase project_ref pinned") in the memory entry.

## Definition of Done
- Memory entry written and grep-able.
- Stale entries cleaned.
- One-line confirmation comment posted here with the entry text and what was removed.

## Rationale
The workspace CLAUDE.md declares a default stack (Next.js + Supabase + Vercel) but there is no ADR documenting WHY this stack was chosen, what alternatives were considered, and under what conditions it should be changed. ADR-002 (DEV-37) exists for the App Hub Model but references a decision (the default stack) that has no ADR. Monthly architecture reviews cannot reliably assess stack drift without a baseline ADR-001.

## Evidence
- Only ADR found: DEV-37 (ADR-002)
- CLAUDE.md states: 'Default stack is Next.js + Supabase + Vercel unless the app demands otherwise'
- No ADR-001 issue exists in '00 - Factory Brain' project
- Monthly arch review window: 2026-04-01 → 2026-05-26

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] ADR-001 issue created in '00 - Factory Brain' project with title 'ADR-001 — Default Tech Stack (Next.js + Supabase + Vercel)'
- [ ] ADR-001 body includes: decision (one paragraph), layer-by-layer rationale, alternatives considered, reversal conditions (when would we migrate away?), current status (adopted)
- [ ] Layers covered: frontend, backend/API, DB, auth, deploy, mobile, payments, messaging, analytics
- [ ] ADR-001 cross-referenced in CLAUDE.md or the solution-architect prompt so future runs cite it

## Dependencies
- None (standalone document)

## Risk
Without ADR-001, every future monthly review starts without a documented baseline; stack drift is invisible and reversal decisions are made without documented rationale.

## Definition of Done
ADR-001 issue exists, is in 'done' status, and is cross-referenced in the architecture review process.

## Effort: S (half day)

## Rationale
CVE-2026-46701 (HIGH — Network-AI: Unauthenticated Cross-Origin MCP Tool Invocation via Empty Default Secret) and CVE-2026-46519 (HIGH — MCP Server Kubernetes: Tool Access Control Bypass via Presentation-Layer Filtering Without Execution-Layer Enforcement) demonstrate that MCP servers with empty or absent secrets allow unauthenticated cross-origin tool invocations. ADR-002 (DEV-37) plans to expose the codegraph layer via MCP (`codegraph_search`, `codegraph_trace`, `codegraph_callers`, `codegraph_impact`). If MCP servers are stood up without execution-layer auth, any cross-tenant or unauthenticated call can invoke codegraph tools.

## Evidence
- CVE-2026-46701: https://github.com/advisories (GitHub Security Advisory — npm ecosystem)
- CVE-2026-46519: https://github.com/advisories (GitHub Security Advisory — npm ecosystem)
- ADR-002 (DEV-37): codegraph MCP integration planned

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A security policy document (or CLAUDE.md addendum) is written specifying: all MCP servers deployed in this workspace MUST have a non-empty shared secret configured; execution-layer auth (not just presentation-layer filtering) must be enforced
- [ ] Policy covers: secret rotation cadence, how to validate auth on each MCP server tool call, logging of rejected attempts
- [ ] Policy is referenced in the `app-hub-bootstrap` skill so every new app that adds MCP tools gets the check automatically
- [ ] Security review sign-off before any codegraph MCP server goes live

## Dependencies
- ADR-002 (DEV-37) codegraph integration
- Workspace CLAUDE.md update rights

## Risk
Unauthenticated cross-origin tool invocation when codegraph MCP goes live; potential data leakage across app tenants.

## Definition of Done
Security policy written and referenced in app-hub-bootstrap skill. No MCP server deployment proceeds without this policy in place.

## Effort: S (1-2 days)

## Skill: parallel-issue-dispatcher

**Skill ID:** `9f4b229b-4f75-454b-9548-2df4e4b80916`
**Source:** DEV-82 — extracted from Cursor "Build in Parallel" + Replit Agent 4 parallel task execution benchmarks (DEV-77, DEV-78).

## Purpose
Auto-identify independent sub-issues within a parent and batch-promote all of them to `--status todo` simultaneously, using a dependency DAG to determine readiness. Eliminates the one-by-one sprint-commander bottleneck.

## Trigger
- Any parent issue with 3+ sub-issues in `backlog` status where at least some are independent.
- Explicitly invoked by sprint-commander or ceo after a parent issue's build plan is finalized.

## Required Input
- `parent_issue_id` — the parent issue whose sub-issues to process.
- `project_id` — the project the parent belongs to (available from `multica issue get <parent-id> --output json` → `.project_id`).
- Sub-issues must have a `Dependencies` field listing referenced issue IDs or `none`.

## Steps
1. Fetch the parent issue to get its `project_id`: `multica issue get <parent-id> --output json`. Then list all issues in that project: `multica issue list --project <project-id> --output json` and filter client-side where `parent_issue_id == <parent-id>`. (Note: `multica issue list` has no `--parent` flag; project-level listing with client-side filter is the correct path. Use `--limit` and `--offset` to paginate if the project has >50 issues.)
2. Parse each sub-issue's `Dependencies` field; treat missing field as independent.
3. Build a DAG; mark sub-issues *ready* when all declared dependencies are `done` or `in_review`.
4. Batch-promote all ready sub-issues to `todo`.
5. Post dispatch-map comment on parent (promoted vs. blocked + reasons).
6. Re-run on each sub-issue `done` event to promote newly unblocked issues.

## Output
- All independent sub-issues promoted to `todo` in one pass.
- Dispatch-map comment on parent issue.

## Quality Checklist
- [x] DAG correctly identifies all independent vs. blocked issues
- [x] No issue promoted if any upstream dependency is not `done` or `in_review`
- [x] Missing `Dependencies` field treated as independent (not an error)
- [x] Parent comment posted with full dispatch map
- [ ] Re-run logic triggers on sub-issue `done` transitions
- [x] No sub-issue promoted more than once per dispatch cycle

## Failure Handling
- CLI failure on fetch → post `blocked` comment, stop.
- Unparseable description → log as "unparseable — treated as independent", promote.
- Single promotion failure → log and continue batch; don't abort.

## Improvement Rule
After 3 uses: measure parallelization rate (independent/total). If < 50%, audit issue-creation practices.
After 5 uses: refine or retire.

## Definition of Done
- [x] Skill spec complete with all required fields
- [x] Registered via `multica skill create`
- [x] Synthetic test passed (QA DEV-82, 2026-05-26): 3 sub-issues (2 independent + 1 blocked), steps 1–5 verified, step 6 deferred to first real use
- [ ] Assigned to sprint-commander agent

## Input
Pattern imported from benchmark sweep: Replit Agent 3/4 autonomous test-fix loop (3× faster, 10× cheaper than Computer Use per Replit's benchmarks).
Source brief: [DEV-78](mention://issue/74a6652c-9293-45ca-b6f6-9ff0e2459a58) (Replit Agent)

## Skill to Build: `autonomous-test-fix-loop`

### Purpose
After a build agent ships a feature and marks it `in_review`, automatically run the QA test plan against the live preview URL, detect failures, and create a self-healing fix sub-issue assigned back to the build agent. Human only reviews when the loop converges or after a configurable max-retry limit.

### Trigger
- A build sub-issue transitions to `in_review`.
- A QA engineer has attached a test plan to the issue.

### Required Input
- Issue ID of the feature being tested
- Preview URL (from issue metadata `deploy_url` or comment)
- QA test plan (from issue description or QA sub-issue)

### Steps
1. Read the QA test plan from the issue.
2. Execute each test case against the preview URL (headless browser / Playwright).
3. If all pass: comment "All tests passed. Ready for QA review." — done.
4. If any fail: create a fix sub-issue (`--status todo`, assigned to original build agent) with the failure log.
5. On fix sub-issue `in_review`: re-run the loop from step 2.
6. After 3 failed retry cycles: escalate to qa-engineer and ceo with full failure log.

### Output
- Pass/fail result comment on the feature issue.
- Fix sub-issue (if failures found).
- Escalation comment after 3 retry failures.

### Quality Checklist
- [ ] Test runner executes all test cases from the QA plan
- [ ] Failure log captures exact assertion + screenshot
- [ ] Fix sub-issue created with correct parent link and build agent assignee
- [ ] Max-retry cap enforced (3 cycles)
- [ ] Escalation posted after cap reached

### Failure Handling
If preview URL is unreachable: post `blocked` comment with reason. Do not create fix sub-issue.

### Improvement Rule
After 5 uses, measure mean cycles-to-green. If > 2, audit QA test plan quality with qa-engineer.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Skill spec written and filed in `08 - Skill Library`
- [ ] skill-builder implements skill
- [ ] Integration test: one build issue with a known failing test → fix loop fires and converges

## Dependencies
- [DEV-62](mention://issue/c289d731-eaf9-4747-814f-c122f1ed08cd) benchmark rollup (this parent)

## Risk
Medium. Creates sub-issues and transitions statuses autonomously — test the retry cap carefully to avoid loops.

## Definition of Done
Skill filed in 08 - Skill Library. Tested. No secrets.

## Input

Source briefs: [DEV-77](mention://issue/e5cf1d27-3c5b-458a-967b-95b4e3f6b017) (Cursor), [DEV-78](mention://issue/74a6652c-9293-45ca-b6f6-9ff0e2459a58) (Replit Agent)

## Skill to Build: `parallel-issue-dispatcher`

### Purpose
Auto-identify independent sub-issues within a parent and batch-promote all of them to `--status todo` simultaneously. Today, the sprint-commander promotes sub-issues one-by-one; this skill reads the dependency graph and launches all non-blocked work in parallel.

### Trigger
- Any parent issue with 3+ sub-issues in `backlog` status where some are independent.
- Explicitly called by sprint-commander or ceo after a parent issue's build plan is finalized.

### Required Input
- Parent issue ID
- List of sub-issues with their declared `Dependencies` field values

### Steps
1. Read all sub-issues of parent. Parse each issue's `Dependencies` field.
2. Build a dependency DAG: which sub-issues have no upstream dependencies (or all upstream are `done`/`in_review`)?
3. Promote all dependency-free sub-issues to `--status todo` in a single batch.
4. Comment on parent: "Dispatched N sub-issues in parallel: [list]. Remaining M are backlog pending: [list]."
5. When any sub-issue reaches `done`, re-run the DAG and promote newly unblocked issues.

### Output
- All independent sub-issues promoted to `todo` in one pass.
- Parent comment with dispatch map.

### Quality Checklist
- [ ] DAG correctly identifies all independent vs. blocked issues
- [ ] No issue promoted if any upstream dependency is not `done` or `in_review`
- [ ] Parent comment posted with full dispatch map
- [ ] Re-run logic triggers on sub-issue completion

### Failure Handling
If a sub-issue has no `Dependencies` field, treat it as independent (safe default).

### Improvement Rule
After 3 uses, measure actual parallelization rate (independent/total). If < 50%, audit issue creation practices.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Skill spec written and filed in `08 - Skill Library`
- [ ] skill-builder implements skill with all required fields
- [ ] Tested against a real parent issue with mixed independent + blocked sub-issues

## Dependencies
- [DEV-62](mention://issue/c289d731-eaf9-4747-814f-c122f1ed08cd) benchmark rollup (this parent)

## Risk
Low. Read-only analysis + status promotion — fully reversible.

## Definition of Done
Skill filed in 08 - Skill Library. Tested. No secrets.

## What It Does

Replit Agent is a cloud-native, full-stack AI software development agent. Describe what you want in natural language; Agent plans, writes, tests, debugs, and deploys the app — autonomously, in a browser, with no local setup. Supports web apps, mobile apps, APIs, automations, slides, and static sites.

---

## Timeline of Major Releases (2025–2026)

| Date | Release | Headline |
|------|---------|----------|

| Dec 2025 | Custom MCP Servers | Connect to remote MCP servers; hundreds of external tools |

## Current State (Agent 4, May 2026)

**Autonomous run length:** Up to 200 minutes uninterrupted.

**What it builds:**

- Automated agents (Slack/email/Telegram bots, scheduled jobs)
- Presentations and static sites

**Backend / Database:**

**Deployment:**

**Collaboration (Agent 4):**

## Replit Agent vs. Ghostwriter (Legacy)

| Starter (Free) | $0 | Limited trial | Up to 10 dev apps; 1 vCPU / 2 GiB RAM |

| Teams | $35–$40/user/mo | $40/mo per user | Shared workspace |
| Pro | $95/mo (annual) | Shared pool | Up to 15 builders; no per-seat fees |

Effort-based pricing (Jun 2025): simple changes ~$0.25/checkpoint; complex tasks bundled into one higher-cost checkpoint. Deployment costs extra.

---

## Key Strengths

1. **Zero local setup** — fully browser-based; works for non-developers ("vibe coding")
2. **Longest autonomous run** — 200 min uninterrupted, self-testing in real browser
3. **Connectors breadth** — 30+ native + unlimited via MCP

5. **Agent-spawning** — can create scheduled automation agents (Slack bots, cron jobs)
6. **Agent 4 parallelism** — multiple artifacts/tasks build simultaneously

## Notable Limits

1. **Vendor lock-in** — cloud-only; migrating complex apps out is non-trivial
2. **Pricing unpredictability** — effort-based credits make large build costs unclear upfront
3. **Not repo-native** — weaker for teams with existing local Git workflows
4. **Complex enterprise features** — CVE patching, SBOM only in Enterprise (pricing unlisted)
5. **Power mode cost** — Claude Opus 4.7 burns credits fast

## Vendor: Cursor (cursor.sh) — Agent Mode Brief
**Date:** 2026-05-26 | **Analyst:** docs-analyst | **Parent:** DEV-62

---

## 1. What Agent Mode Does Autonomously

**Core capabilities:**
- **Multi-file edits in one pass** — understands file relationships; adding an API endpoint automatically updates routes, types, and tests
- **Terminal command execution** — runs shell commands, installs packages, executes tests inside the working environment
- **Web browsing** — generates search queries, browses docs/Stack Overflow/API references to resolve unknowns at runtime
- **Computer Use (since Feb 24, 2026)** — cloud agents get a full desktop+browser VM; they open browsers, navigate to localhost, click through UI to visually verify their own code changes
- **Background/Cloud Agents** — async cloud VMs with ephemeral repo checkouts; takes a GitHub issue or Slack message → produces a draft PR without the developer's machine being open
- **Multi-repo parallel execution** — Agents Window surfaces all local + cloud agents in sidebar; launch agents across mobile, web, desktop, Slack, GitHub, Linear simultaneously
- **Multitask/async subagents** (`/multitask`, added Apr 24, 2026) — spawns isolated subagents per independent task branch instead of queuing serially
- **BugBot** — automated PR reviewer; by Feb 2026 upgraded to auto-fixer with ~80% resolution rate

---

## 2. Recent Major Features / Changelog (2025–2026)

| Date | Version | Key Change |
|------|---------|-----------|
| Oct 2025 | 2.0 | Composer model launched (4x faster, <30s per turn); Background Agents GA; Memories (persistent project context); Jupyter support; one-click MCP |
| Mar 2026 | 3.0 | Full rebuild from scratch, agent-first interface; Agents Window (parallel agent mgmt); Cursor Marketplace (plugins); cloud↔local handoff; multi-repo; Design Mode (annotate browser UI for agents) |
| Apr 24, 2026 | 3.x | `/multitask` async subagents; 35% of Cursor's own PRs now authored by cloud agents |
| May 13, 2026 | 3.4 | Full-screen tabs for files/canvases/PRs/terminals; compact chat response density controls |
| May 18, 2026 | — | Composer 2.5 (better long-running task endurance, instruction-following) |
| May 19, 2026 | — | Jira integration — assign to @Cursor or mention in comments; agent delivers PR link |
| May 20, 2026 | 3.5 | Shared Canvases (shareable live agent artifacts); Automations multi-repo; non-code automations (Slack digests, finance tracking) |

---

## 3. Pricing Tiers (as of May 2026)

| Plan | Price | Key Limits |
|------|-------|-----------|
| Hobby | Free | Limited agent requests, limited Tab completions |
| Pro | $20/mo | Unlimited Tab completions, standard agent request pool, $20 model credit |
| Pro+ | $60/mo | 3x credit pool vs Pro |
| Ultra | $200/mo | 20x usage multiplier, priority feature access |
| Teams | $40/user/mo | Everything in Pro + shared rules/chats, SSO/SAML, RBAC, centralized billing |
| Enterprise | Custom | Custom SLA, security controls |

Composer 2.5 model token pricing: Standard $0.50/$2.50 per M in/out; Fast (default) $3.00/$15.00 per M in/out.

---

## 4. Plugin / Extensions Ecosystem

**Cursor Marketplace** (launched Mar 2026):
- Consolidates MCPs, skills, subagents, rules, and hooks into a single in-IDE surface
- One-click install; private team marketplace option for Enterprise
- Notable integrations: Amplitude, AWS, Figma, Linear, Stripe, Jira (native), Slack (native), GitHub (native)
- VS Code extensions still fully supported (it's a VS Code fork)
- 30+ new plugins added in first wave post-launch

---

## 5. Key Strengths

1. **Tab completions** — proprietary specialized model; real-time inline prediction; no comparable CLI tool has this
2. **Visual inline diffs** — line-level change review, staged approval; superior UX for humans reviewing agent output
3. **Multi-model flexibility** — switch GPT-5.3, Claude, Gemini within one session
4. **Computer Use agents** — visual browser verification of generated UI is a genuine differentiator
5. **Design Mode** — annotate browser UI elements directly for agent consumption
6. **IDE familiarity** — VS Code fork means zero editor relearning for most devs
7. **Team pricing** — $40/user vs Claude Code's $125/user for teams

---

## 6. Notable Limits / Weaknesses

- **Context window degradation** — advertises 200K tokens but real usable window is reported at 70K–120K in practice
- **VS Code lock-in** — can't use outside of their fork; architectural decisions increasingly diverge from upstream
- **Cost at scale** — token consumption per agent run reported 12–80% higher than CLI alternatives; potential 10x price difference vs Claude Code for identical workflows
- **Conversational-only memory model** — no durable artifact-based requirements tracking; rules/prompt files are its only persistence mechanism
- **Community tension** — Cursor 3's IDE-to-agent pivot alienated a segment of power users who valued tight synchronous editing control
- **Tightening limits** — usage caps reported to tighten quarterly

---

## 7. Competitive Position Summary

Cursor leads on **developer UX, tab completions, visual diff review, and GUI-based agent verification**. It competes on breadth of model access and team pricing. Its weakest flanks relative to this workspace's stack are **effective context window size** and **cost per agent run at scale** — both relevant for high-volume App Factory workflows.

---

## Acceptance
Brief filed per DEV-62 mandate. Part of six-vendor benchmark sweep.

Monthly Architecture & Tool-stack Review — keeps ADRs current, default tech stack honest, and per-integration posture fresh. Runs on the 1st of each month at 11:00 Asia/Kuwait (GMT+3) — Multica's cron parser does not support "nth weekday of month", so day-1 was chosen over "every Sunday + self-gate" to avoid spawning 3 auto-cancelled issues per month. Mode=create_issue (project: 00 - Factory Brain, id 1a1c2fb3-41ee-4b99-b820-d10f1c564052).

CRITICAL SAFETY RULES:
- Mode is create_issue. Do NOT create additional issues except the up-to-5 architecture-action sub-issues in step (7).
- NEVER mention the CEO (d9f942b9-e245-4e28-99eb-8f12fbebc2c3) in any comment — assignment alone is the trigger.
- NEVER mention any agent in a wrap-up or thank-you. Only mention an agent on dispatch sub-issues, and only if assignment alone is insufficient.
- NEVER touch DEV-36 (the workspace-operations dialog).
- Read each issue's metadata before pinning anything; do not log run state.

REVIEW WINDOW:
- `since = first day of previous calendar month, 00:00 Asia/Kuwait` → `now`. Use RFC3339 UTC for any CLI/API filter.

INPUTS TO PULL (continue on per-source failure — log "skipped <source>: <reason>" to stdout):
- All ADRs in `00 - Factory Brain` (title prefix "ADR-" or label "adr"). `multica issue list --project 1a1c2fb3-41ee-4b99-b820-d10f1c564052 --output json`.
- All apps shipped or in-flight in the window (projects whose titles start with "APP —").
- Default stack ground-truth: scan `multica skill list --output json` plus the workspace CLAUDE.md content surfaced in your runtime brief.
- Per-runtime cost / quota notes: latest "Workspace Upgrade Weekly Loop" issue + comments for the trailing month (project 00 - Factory Brain).
- Per-integration health: upstream release notes via `gh` CLI / public RSS / vendor changelogs. Never use `curl`/`wget` against Multica.
- Open CVEs: GitHub Security Advisories (`gh api /advisories?ecosystem=...`) for Node, Go, Postgres, Next.js, Expo, plus declared SDKs.

STEPS:

(1) ADR audit
   - List every ADR-* issue (any status).
   - For each, classify: still-load-bearing | superseded (cite successor) | contradicted (cite the shipped app / decision that diverged) | stale.
   - Cross-reference each shipped app's PRD / schema / API contract to detect contradictions.
   - Output as a table.

(2) Default tech stack
   - Restate the current default stack per layer (frontend, backend, DB, auth, deploy, mobile, payments, messaging, analytics).
   - For each layer: (a) what matured in the past 30 days (releases, GA milestones), (b) any alternative now worth a re-look, (c) recommendation: hold | watch | re-evaluate.

(3) Per-runtime delta
   - Runtimes in scope: Claude Code, Codex, Opencode, Openclaw, Hermes.
   - For each: pricing / quota changes, new capabilities, deprecations, observed failure rate this month (cite any `agent-self-healing-policy` retry_log surfaced on issues).

(4) Per-integration delta
   - Integrations: Supabase, Vercel, KNet, MyFatoorah, Meta (FB/IG/WhatsApp Graph), Google (Maps/OAuth/Workspace), Expo.
   - For each: pricing changes, breaking API changes, new quotas / rate limits, deprecation notices. One bullet per integration. If nothing landed, write "no changes".

(5) Security posture
   - New CVE classes affecting our stack.
   - RLS / secret-handling gaps surfaced this month (cross-reference any `security-review-checklist` failures in `06 - QA Security Performance`).
   - One-paragraph posture summary + bulleted gaps.

(6) Top 5 architecture changes to consider
   - Derive from steps (1)–(5). Rank by impact × urgency. For each: title, rationale (1–2 lines), owner (squad / agent), effort (S/M/L), risk if skipped.
   - List fewer than 5 if fewer are warranted — do NOT invent fillers.
   - If the month is genuinely quiet, list 0 actions and post the comment marker "no changes — green".

(7) Auto-dispatch the Top 5 (or fewer) as sub-issues
   - For each action, create a sub-issue:
     - Title: "ARCH ACTION: <one-line>"
     - Parent: this autopilot's issue id
     - Project: 00 - Factory Brain (1a1c2fb3-41ee-4b99-b820-d10f1c564052)
     - Priority: high for security / CVE; medium for stack or runtime drift; low for "watch" items
     - Status: todo
     - Assignee: solution-architect (77da9a0d-924e-4087-9278-59ba1182b599) by default; database items → database-architect (89c4ed8b-e38b-48c4-9029-19fd7007a255); API contract items → api-architect (614bc86d-6ef1-4f1e-8b3c-b393b43f46d0); integration items → integration-architect (4d62192f-7508-4ed2-99f5-db8b3dabbd9c); security items → security-auditor (17759d29-f9bb-4c4c-910c-b4476723db87).
     - Description includes: rationale, evidence (links to ADR / app / CVE / release notes), Acceptance Criteria, DoD, suggested effort.
   - If the month is "green" (0 actions), skip dispatch but still complete steps (8)–(10).

(8) Post the audit report as ONE comment on this autopilot's created issue. Use this exact shape (HEREDOC via `multica issue comment add <this-issue-id> --content-stdin`):

```
# Monthly Architecture & Tool-stack Review — <YYYY-MM>

**Window:** <since> → <now> (Asia/Kuwait)
**Apps reviewed:** <N> shipped, <N> in-flight
**ADRs reviewed:** <N>

## ADR audit

| --- | --- | --- |

## Per-integration

## Security posture
<one paragraph + bulleted gaps, or "no new CVE classes; no RLS/secret gaps surfaced this month">

## Top 5 architecture changes
1. **<title>** — owner: <agent/squad> | effort: S/M/L | risk-if-skipped: <one line>
2. …

(If the month is quiet: "no changes — green")

## Dispatched sub-issues
- DEV-NN — <ARCH ACTION title>
- …
```

Do NOT include any `mention://agent/...` link in this comment.

(9) Pin metadata on this issue ONLY when materially useful for future runs. Recommended keys:
   - `review_window_end` (RFC3339)
   - `adrs_reviewed` (number)
   - `apps_reviewed` (number)
   - `actions_dispatched` (number, 0–5)
   - `posture` (string: green | yellow | red)
   Use `multica issue metadata set` once each; skip any key whose value matches what `metadata list` already shows.

(10) Flip this issue's status to `in_review` (`multica issue status <this-issue-id> in_review`). Do NOT close it — the CEO Command Squad confirms Architecture Squad has actioned the dispatched sub-issues. Sub-issues run on their own assignees.

OUTPUT STDOUT (one line only, no extra comment):
`Monthly Arch Review @ <RFC3339> | adrs=N | apps=N | actions=N | posture=<green|yellow|red>`

OPERATING RULES:
- Never block on a single CLI / `gh` / HTTP failure. Log "skipped <source>: <reason>" and continue.
- Even an empty month gets a "no changes — green" comment — never silent.
- Cap dispatch at 5 sub-issues hard.
- Read metadata before pinning. Only update keys that changed since last review.

Skill `self-hosted-supabase-tunnel`. Input: schema files + seed data + a free Cloudflare account (or ngrok fallback). Steps:
1. Pull official Supabase docker-compose recipe
2. Persist data to `/opt/data/supabase-<project>`
3. Apply migrations + seed
4. Bring up `cloudflared` tunnel (free, no card); fallback `ngrok` if cloudflared unavailable
5. Emit `demo_backend_url` to caller as metadata
6. Document restart procedure (volumes survive runtime restart)
7. Mark url + creds as DEMO ONLY in any artifact that embeds them

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] First call brings a live backend in ≤ 10 minutes
- [ ] Idempotent: re-run uses existing tunnel if alive
- [ ] No paid resource required

## DoD
Skill published. Referenced from `one-prompt-to-app` as the default demo path when production credentials are not yet provisioned.
issues.title: SKILL: self-hosted-supabase-tunnel — lift DEV-42's demo-backend recipe (docker+cloudflared)

issues.description:
## Input
The agentic ecosystem is moving fast (MCP, Anthropic skills, awesome-claude-code-*). We have no rigorous evaluator for new MCP servers, which means we either ignore them or import them blind.

## Expected Output
Skill `mcp-server-evaluator`. Input: GitHub URL of an MCP server. Output: a one-page evaluation:
- Purpose + capabilities surfaced
- Maintainer + recent activity (last 90 days)
- Permissions / capabilities requested (filesystem, network, secrets)
- Failure modes / known issues
- Security review: secrets handling, input validation, sandbox needs
- Decision: import / adapt / recreate / study only / reject — with reason
- If import: target agent(s) + minimum config + risk-mitigation steps

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Always includes a security review section
- [ ] Always includes a "what breaks if this server goes offline" note
- [ ] Outputs the exact `multica` command to wire the MCP server to an agent (if approved)

## DoD
Skill published. First run completes against a real MCP server.
issues.title: SKILL: mcp-server-evaluator — import/adapt/reject decision with security review

issues.description:
## Input
`nextjs-app-starter` + `supabase-app-starter` exist as separate skills. Every new app re-stitches them. ADR-001 picked Next.js + Supabase as the default stack — the bundle should be a single skill.

## Expected Output
Skill `nextjs-supabase-builder`. Input: app slug + initial schema + role matrix. Steps:
1. Run `nextjs-app-starter` (TS strict, app router, Tailwind, shadcn/ui, next-intl)
2. Run `supabase-app-starter` (Postgres + Auth + Storage + Edge Functions)
3. Wire RLS policies per role matrix
4. Generate env-var checklist (separates client-safe vs server-only)
5. Seed bilingual i18n strings (EN + AR-KW)
6. Add CI: typecheck + lint + test on every PR

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Single command produces a runnable project in ≤ 10 minutes
- [ ] RLS verified on at least one user-scoped table
- [ ] No secrets in client bundle
- [ ] Lighthouse mobile ≥ 90 on the default home page

## DoD
Skill published, referenced by `one-prompt-to-app` for any web build that doesn't pick a different stack.
issues.title: SKILL: nextjs-supabase-builder — combined stack scaffold (NX+SB+RLS+i18n+CI)

issues.description:
## Input
Multiple recent builds blocked on external credentials (DEV-25 → Firebase, DEV-39 → GitHub repo, DEV-35 → FCM project). Each block was escalated ad-hoc, costing hours. Need a single skill that produces one consolidated escalation message for admin@newtechkw.com.

## Expected Output
Skill `external-blocker-escalation`:
- Input: parent issue ID
- Output: one comment with — what is blocked, what credential/resource is needed, exact provisioning steps (with vendor URL), security/cost implications, the free-tier fallback path if user delays
- Auto-pins `waiting_on=admin:<resource>` metadata
- Sends a single `[@admin](mention://member/0c06df4b-79c1-44d4-a0a0-a822d82ae6f4)` mention (only place a member mention is allowed)
- Stops further pulse retries until metadata `waiting_on` is cleared

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Idempotent — re-running on the same issue updates, does not duplicate
- [ ] Free-tier fallback section always present (proves we can keep moving)
- [ ] Used by Factory Pulse v3 step (10 new) — auto-fires when retry_log ≥ 3 with an external-cred error class

## DoD
Skill published, integrated into Factory Pulse, applied retroactively to DEV-25/DEV-35/DEV-39/DEV-40.
issues.title: SKILL: external-blocker-escalation — single-shot consolidated ask to admin

issues.description:
## Goal (single sentence)
Ship the user a downloadable, installable **`.apk` that runs on a clean Android device today**, backed by a self-hosted demo Supabase + stubbed FCM, so [admin@newtechkw.com](mention://member/0c06df4b-79c1-44d4-a0a0-a822d82ae6f4) can perform full UI / offline / RTL / golden-path testing without waiting for paid resources.

## Why now
User asked on [DEV-36](mention://issue/e60d5be5-b9dd-48aa-b949-28286ea80f1b) (parent comment `8f6ce1bf`) for the complete app ready for full Android testing **now**. DEV-33 is code-done, QA-gate-passed (13/13 tests), but no APK exists yet. External-credential ask is in flight in parallel; this issue is the **action path that does NOT wait on the user**.

## Inputs (already available)
- React Native Expo source from [DEV-33](mention://issue/f76d10de-667b-48f8-ae1b-d3baa473fee5) (in mobile-engineer's last worktree — re-checkout from the App Hub project `09e6cfd3-d45e-4225-a494-2e3400b6983c` if cleared)
- Schema + migrations from [DEV-31](mention://issue/89749693-1fd3-42ff-a0bd-481b3feadc76)
- Edge Functions from [DEV-32](mention://issue/bb36dbf3-e989-4e10-bf4e-f0756c5eef37)
- HR Admin Web from [DEV-34](mention://issue/3f3a89bb-3322-4f87-bd97-f6e89cf63d9b) (separate preview URL acceptable)
- Deploy plan from [DEV-39](mention://issue/b2fc5cb4-f977-421f-95be-6d9e18738b22) `DEPLOY.md` + `RUNBOOK.md`

## Expected Output
1. **`attendance-demo-v0.1.0.apk`** — debug-signed, sideloadable, installable on Android API 24+.
   - Embedded `SUPABASE_URL` + `SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` pointing at a **self-hosted Supabase instance** (Docker compose on this runtime host, exposed via Cloudflare tunnel — no paid account needed).
   - FCM stubbed (`FCM_ENABLED=false`) → notifications surface as in-app toasts; no Firebase project required.
   - Embedded demo seed: 1 tenant (شركة الديوان للتجارة), 3 offices (Kuwait City / Salmiya / Hawalli), 5 shifts, 10 employees, 1 super_admin + 2 hr_manager + 7 employees with pre-set passwords.
2. **`HOW_TO_INSTALL.ar.md`** — Kuwaiti Arabic walkthrough: enable "تطبيقات من مصادر غير معروفة", install APK, login as either `super_admin@diwan.demo / Demo2026!` or `employee01@diwan.demo / Demo2026!`, click through full golden path.
3. **Admin web preview URL** — Vercel preview (`vercel deploy` without `--prod`, free tier OK with existing account or a fresh email; if blocked, fall back to ngrok of local `pnpm dev`).
4. **Self-hosted Supabase backend URL** posted as `demo_backend_url` metadata on this issue. Must stay live ≥ 7 days for user testing.
5. **APK attached** to this issue as a file, plus a public download mirror (Cloudflare R2 / temp file.io / GitHub release on `adminnewtech` if PAT exists) for easy phone download.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] APK installs on a clean Android emulator (API 24+) without errors
- [ ] Login → Check-In (GPS happy path with seeded office geofence) → Check-Out → History → Leave Request all work end-to-end
- [ ] Offline test passes: airplane mode → punch → reconnect → syncs without duplicate
- [ ] RTL Arabic Kuwaiti layout is the default on first launch
- [ ] Admin web preview URL is reachable and HR manager seed account can log in, see today's punches, approve a leave
- [ ] `HOW_TO_INSTALL.ar.md` is genuinely Kuwaiti tone (not MSA), tested by [@arabic-rtl-experience-agent](mention://agent/7bcef3ff-a4d6-4ea3-866e-bdcc00b27576)
- [ ] No secrets in the APK, source, or any committed artifact — only demo credentials labelled as demo

## Build approach (recommended)
- **No EAS / no Expo cloud build.** Use `expo prebuild` → local `./gradlew assembleRelease` with debug keystore (`-keystore debug.keystore -keyalg RSA -keysize 2048 -validity 10000 -keypass android -storepass android -dname "CN=Demo"`). Install Android SDK 34 + build-tools 34.0.0 + platform-tools via `sdkmanager` if not present on the runtime host (one-time cost).
- **Self-hosted Supabase via `supabase/postgres` + `supabase/gotrue` + `supabase/postgrest` + `supabase/storage-api` containers**, all from the official `docker-compose` recipe. Exposed via Cloudflare tunnel (free, no card required).
- **Apply migrations from [DEV-31](mention://issue/89749693-1fd3-42ff-a0bd-481b3feadc76) and Edge Functions from [DEV-32](mention://issue/bb36dbf3-e989-4e10-bf4e-f0756c5eef37) on the self-hosted instance**, then seed with `seed.sql` (deliverable in this task).

## Dependencies
None blocking on user. All work happens on this runtime host or free-tier infra.

## Risk
- Self-hosted Supabase will not survive a runtime restart unless persisted to /opt/data — mount volumes and document the restart procedure in `RUNBOOK.md`.
- Cloudflare tunnel free tier requires a `cloudflared` daemon — if not installable, fall back to `ngrok` (also free with a temp account).
- APK signed with debug keystore CANNOT be uploaded to Play Store — that's fine, this is for sideloading only. Mark APK filename and the install guide with **"DEMO BUILD — NOT FOR PLAY STORE"**.

## Definition of Done
APK link posted as a top-level comment on **[DEV-36](mention://issue/e60d5be5-b9dd-48aa-b949-28286ea80f1b)** (the user's parent thread) with: install instructions in Kuwaiti Arabic, demo accounts list, backend URL, preview URL for admin web, a 90-second test checklist, and a known-limitations note. `pipeline_status=demo-apk-ready` pinned on DEV-25 metadata once delivered.

## Coordination
- **deployment-engineer** (assignee) owns: self-hosted Supabase + Cloudflare tunnel + seed data + admin web preview URL + APK hosting mirror.
- **mobile-engineer** (sub-task or peer dispatch): the actual APK build — `expo prebuild` → gradle assembleRelease — wired to the backend URL deployment-engineer provisions.
- Coordinate via this thread; deployment-engineer pings mobile-engineer with the backend URL as soon as it's live so the APK build can embed it.

Self-heal: if `gradle assembleRelease` fails twice with the same error on this runtime, swap to `expo-cli` cloud build (anonymous EAS) before escalating. Document the swap in `retry_log` metadata.
issues.title: Sideloadable Demo APK + Self-hosted Demo Backend — Android Attendance App (Ready-to-test)

issues.description:
## Input
Parent: [DEV-25](mention://issue/f01f5264-86df-4f80-a2ff-f6a1abbb0794). All build sub-issues (DEV-31..35).

This is the **full-app QA test plan** — covers the integration of all builds, not the per-build unit tests (those landed in DEV-31..35 individually).

## Expected Output

A `qa-test-plan.md` in the repo with:

1. **Golden path E2E**: employee logs in → checks in (with geofence + selfie) → checks out → sees correct hours → submits leave request → HR approves → employee receives push → employee sees decision in app.
2. **Anti-golden path**:
   - Outside geofence → check-in rejected with Arabic error
   - Rate-limit on punch endpoint (1 per 30s) blocks fast double-punch
   - Offline mode queues punch → reconnects → syncs without duplicate
   - JWT expired mid-session → app refreshes silently (no re-login UX)
   - Civil ID masked correctly per role
3. **Permission matrix tests**: each of the 4 roles attempts every action; row × column = pass/fail
4. **RLS smoke**: no cross-tenant data leak (tenant A signing in cannot see tenant B's employees)
5. **WCAG 2.2 AA**: keyboard nav on admin dashboard, ARIA labels, color contrast, RTL active
6. **Performance**:
   - p95 punch latency ≤ 800ms
   - Live board update ≤ 3s after punch event
   - Monthly report (500 employees × 31 days) generates < 30s
7. **Security checks** (top up the per-build gates):
   - SQL injection on every parameterised endpoint
   - XSS on every text input rendered in the admin dashboard
   - CSRF / replay attack on punch endpoint with stolen JWT
   - FCM token rotation honoured (old token deleted on rotation)
8. **Device matrix**: Android 7 / 10 / 13 / 14 + emulator + at least one physical device

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Each test has clear Given/When/Then + expected pass criteria
- [ ] Each test mapped to the specific Acceptance Criterion in its source issue (DEV-31..35)
- [ ] Section "Manual smoke tests" lists the 5 minute post-deploy check
- [ ] Plan is executable by a non-author (clear, no assumed context)

## Dependencies
DEV-33 (mobile build) and DEV-34 (admin dashboard) must be ready to install/access before EXECUTION. Plan AUTHORING can start now.

## Risk
- Physical Android device for testing — flag if not available (emulator coverage is acceptable for MVP per PRD).
- Firebase provisioning still pending → push tests in section 1 cannot execute until DEV-35 Firebase Vault is loaded.

## Definition of Done
`qa-test-plan.md` committed. Comment on this issue with file link + summary of coverage map. Plan reviewed and signed off by [@quality-gate-manager](mention://agent/d87c831d-bd4d-459f-9672-d633e238c44f).
issues.title: Full-App QA Test Plan + E2E — Android Attendance App

issues.description:
## Input
PRD: [DEV-26](mention://issue/799e091f-184e-4551-8f8d-fd55cf62c59f). Architecture: [DEV-30](mention://issue/6d29ecb8-4487-4e92-a87b-cd672b72e219). Schema: [DEV-31](mention://issue/89749693-1fd3-42ff-a0bd-481b3feadc76). API: [DEV-32](mention://issue/bb36dbf3-e989-4e10-bf4e-f0756c5eef37).

## Expected Output
Next.js 14+ (App Router) admin web dashboard deployed on Vercel:

**Pages / Features**:
1. Login — Supabase Auth email + password; session via @supabase/auth-helpers-nextjs
2. Live Attendance Board — real-time grid via Supabase Realtime WebSocket; status badges (حاضر / متأخر / غائب / إجازة); department filter; auto-refresh ≤3s latency
3. Employee Management — data table with search/filter; create (with civil ID input, encrypted before save), edit, deactivate; photo upload to Supabase Storage
4. Office Management — CRUD with interactive map (Google Maps or Leaflet); geofence radius slider; GPS coordinate input
5. Shift Templates — CRUD form: name, start, end, grace minutes, break minutes; shift assignment to employees
6. Reports — date range picker; employee / department filter; CSV download (direct) + PDF download (async, signed URL); civil ID masked for non-HR-Manager roles
7. Leave Approval — pending requests queue; approve / reject with optional note; filtered by dept for Dept Manager
8. Audit Log — paginated table (100/page); filters: actor, date range, action type; CSV export

**Technical requirements**:
- Next.js 14 App Router + TypeScript
- @supabase/ssr for server-side Supabase client (RLS-enforced queries)
- Supabase Realtime client for live board WebSocket subscription
- Tailwind CSS + shadcn/ui component library
- React Query (TanStack) for client-side data fetching
- react-hook-form + zod for form validation
- i18n: next-intl, Arabic Kuwaiti default, English toggle, RTL via dir='rtl' on <html>
- Role-based route guards: middleware.ts checks JWT role claim; redirects unauthorized access
- Civil ID: never stored or displayed in plaintext on frontend — always masked (****XXXX) except in HR Manager employee detail page where full value fetched via Edge Function
- WCAG 2.2 AA: keyboard navigation, ARIA labels, sufficient contrast

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Live board updates within 3 seconds of a punch event via Supabase Realtime
- [ ] Dept Manager sees only own department employees on live board and leave queue
- [ ] Civil ID displayed as ****XXXX for all roles except hr_manager/super_admin viewing employee detail
- [ ] Monthly report (500 employees, 31 days) generates and starts download within 30 seconds
- [ ] Route guard blocks Employee role from accessing /admin/* routes
- [ ] RTL layout active when Arabic locale is selected
- [ ] Deployed to Vercel with preview URL

## Dependencies
Supabase project URL + anon key (public) + service_role key (Vercel env only). DEV-31 (schema). DEV-32 (API Edge Functions for reports, invite). Google Maps API key for office geofence map.

## Risk
- Google Maps API key requires billing account — use Leaflet + OpenStreetMap as fallback for MVP.
- PDF report generation timeout on large datasets — trigger async job (202 response) and poll for signed URL.

## Definition of Done
All 8 feature areas implemented. Deployed to Vercel preview URL. Happy path tested: create employee → assign shift → employee punches in → live board shows update → HR runs monthly report → downloads CSV.
issues.title: HR Admin Web Dashboard — Next.js (Attendance App)
